stem: A 26-year-old woman presents to the emergency department with severe lower abdominal pain and collapse at home. She is approximately 8 weeks pregnant based on her last menstrual period and had attended a GP 3 days earlier for light vaginal spotting. At that time her serum beta-hCG was 2100 IU/L and transvaginal ultrasound showed no intrauterine pregnancy but a possible right adnexal lesion. She was scheduled for repeat testing today. Instead, she developed sudden right-sided abdominal pain radiating to the shoulder tip followed by dizziness and near-syncope. On arrival she is pale, diaphoretic, and intermittently confused. Abdominal examination reveals marked lower abdominal tenderness with guarding. A bedside FAST scan demonstrates free fluid in the pelvis and Morrison pouch. The emergency registrar asks whether methotrexate could still be considered because the earlier beta-hCG level was relatively low.
lead_in: What is the most appropriate immediate next step in management?

options.text: Administer methotrexate because haemodynamic instability does not alter management when beta-hCG is low
options.rationale: Methotrexate is contraindicated in suspected ruptured ectopic pregnancy with haemodynamic instability. Surgical management is required. (Source: KEMH Ectopic Pregnancy Guideline)

options.text: Repeat transvaginal ultrasound because FAST scanning may overestimate physiologic pelvic free fluid
options.rationale: This patient is in haemorrhagic shock with clinical evidence of rupture. Delaying operative management for repeat imaging would be unsafe. (Source: RANZCOG Early Pregnancy Loss)

options.text: Commence urgent resuscitation and proceed to emergency surgical management
options.rationale: This presentation is classic for ruptured ectopic pregnancy causing haemorrhagic shock. Immediate resuscitation and operative intervention are required. (Source: KEMH Ectopic Pregnancy Guideline)

options.text: Observe after fluid resuscitation because spontaneous tubal abortion may stabilise without intervention
options.rationale: Haemodynamic instability and intraperitoneal bleeding indicate life-threatening rupture requiring definitive surgical management. (Source: RANZCOG Early Pregnancy Loss)

options.text: Arrange urgent CT abdomen because appendicitis remains an important differential diagnosis
options.rationale: Although abdominal pain differentials exist, the clinical picture overwhelmingly supports ruptured ectopic pregnancy and requires immediate treatment. (Source: KEMH Ectopic Pregnancy Guideline)

stem: A 33-year-old primigravida at 35 weeks gestation presents with worsening frontal headache and visual blurring over the past 12 hours. Her pregnancy has been complicated by gestational hypertension diagnosed at 31 weeks. She reports increasing upper abdominal discomfort and nausea today. Foetal movements remain present. On review of antenatal records, foetal growth was on the 12th percentile at the most recent ultrasound. In the assessment unit she appears uncomfortable and photophobic. Reflexes are brisk. CTG demonstrates a reactive trace without decelerations. The junior resident asks whether antihypertensive adjustment alone is sufficient because the foetal status currently appears reassuring.
lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.text: Increase oral antihypertensive therapy and continue expectant management because CTG remains reassuring
options.rationale: This patient has severe pre-eclampsia features including neurological symptoms, severe hypertension, thrombocytopenia, and liver involvement requiring escalation beyond oral antihypertensives alone. (Source: SOMANZ Hypertension Guideline)

options.text: Commence magnesium sulfate and plan expedited delivery after maternal stabilisation
options.rationale: Severe pre-eclampsia with neurological symptoms and end-organ involvement requires seizure prophylaxis and delivery planning after stabilisation. (Source: SOMANZ Hypertension Guideline)

options.text: Administer corticosteroids and delay delivery for at least 7 days because prematurity risk outweighs maternal concerns
options.rationale: Maternal severe disease at 35 weeks generally outweighs benefits of prolonged expectant management. (Source: RANZCOG Hypertensive Disorders)

options.text: Arrange outpatient blood pressure review tomorrow because foetal wellbeing remains reassuring
options.rationale: This degree of hypertension and end-organ involvement requires inpatient urgent management rather than outpatient follow-up. (Source: SOMANZ Hypertension Guideline)

options.text: Commence intravenous antibiotics because thrombocytopenia and transaminitis suggest occult sepsis
options.rationale: The overall pattern strongly supports severe pre-eclampsia rather than primary sepsis. (Source: RANZCOG Hypertensive Disorders)

stem: A 31-year-old multiparous woman undergoes spontaneous vaginal delivery of a healthy infant at 39 weeks gestation after induction for gestational diabetes. The placenta delivers intact 10 minutes later. Shortly afterwards she develops ongoing heavy vaginal bleeding with large clots. Uterine massage is attempted while additional staff are called. She received oxytocin during labour augmentation. The registrar notes the uterus feels enlarged and boggy above the umbilicus. There is no obvious genital tract trauma on initial inspection. A junior doctor suggests waiting briefly because the patient remains awake and conversational.
lead_in: What is the most appropriate immediate next intervention?

options.text: Perform immediate hysterectomy because severe postpartum bleeding rarely responds to medical management
options.rationale: Hysterectomy may become necessary later but initial management of uterine atony involves uterotonics and resuscitative measures. (Source: RANZCOG Postpartum Haemorrhage)

options.text: Commence uterotonic therapy while continuing uterine massage and haemodynamic resuscitation
options.rationale: The boggy enlarged uterus strongly suggests uterine atony, which requires prompt uterotonics, massage, and resuscitation. (Source: RANZCOG Postpartum Haemorrhage)

options.text: Observe for spontaneous uterine contraction because the patient remains conscious and responsive
options.rationale: Ongoing heavy bleeding with haemodynamic compromise requires urgent active management rather than observation. (Source: RWH Postpartum Haemorrhage Guideline)

options.text: Arrange urgent CT angiography because retained products are the most likely cause of bleeding
options.rationale: The boggy uterus and immediate postpartum timing strongly support uterine atony rather than a primary need for imaging. (Source: RANZCOG Postpartum Haemorrhage)

options.text: Prioritise broad-spectrum antibiotics because postpartum endometritis commonly causes major haemorrhage
options.rationale: This acute heavy postpartum bleeding pattern with uterine atony is not typical of endometritis. (Source: RWH Postpartum Haemorrhage Guideline)

stem: A 32-year-old G2P1 at 38+2 weeks gestation presents in spontaneous labour. Her pregnancy has been uncomplicated. On admission 30 minutes ago, cervical examination was 5 cm dilated with intact membranes, cephalic presentation at station -1. She is now reclining in bed and reports a sudden gush of clear fluid followed by a sensation of something in her vagina. On examination, you palpate a pulsatile, soft, looped structure presenting at the introitus. The foetal heart rate, which was previously 140 bpm with good variability, is now 70 bpm and has remained there for 90 seconds despite repositioning the woman into left lateral. Tocolysis with subcutaneous terbutaline 250 mcg has been administered. The obstetric registrar and theatre team have been called and are 5 minutes away. The woman is visibly distressed and repeatedly asks if her baby will be okay.
lead_in: What is the SINGLE MOST appropriate next step while awaiting theatre?

options.text: Perform a digital vaginal examination to assess cervical dilation and exclude footling breech
options.rationale: Digital examination of a cord prolapse risks further cord compression, spasm, and worsens foetal hypoxia. The priority is continuous elevation of the presenting part without manipulating the cord. (Source: RANZCOG: Cord Prolapse)

options.text: Elevate the presenting part continuously with a hand in the vagina and fill the bladder with 500–700 mL of normal saline via catheter
options.rationale: Continuous manual elevation of the presenting part off the cord combined with rapid tocolysis and bladder filling (retrograde filling of the bladder) is the definitive temporising measure to reduce cord compression while preparing for immediate delivery. The presenting part is lifted cephalad and the distended bladder helps maintain that displacement. (Source: RANZCOG: Cord Prolapse; KEMH Clinical Guidelines: Cord Prolapse)

options.text: Administer a second dose of terbutaline 250 mcg SC and place the woman in the Trendelenburg position
options.rationale: Tocolysis has already been administered, and a second dose is unlikely to improve the situation and may cause maternal tachycardia without foetal benefit. Trendelenburg position alone is insufficient to relieve cord compression. (Source: RANZCOG: Cord Prolapse)

options.text: Prompt the woman to push actively to deliver the foetus as quickly as possible vaginally
options.rationale: The cervix is 6 cm dilated and the presenting part is at station -2; vaginal delivery is not imminent. Encouraging pushing will worsen cord compression by driving the presenting part downward. The priority is emergency caesarean section after cord decompression measures. (Source: RANZCOG: Cord Prolapse)

options.text: Attempt manual replacement of the cord into the uterine cavity to restore foetal circulation
options.rationale: Manual replacement of a prolapsed cord is no longer recommended as it is rarely successful, traumatises the cord, and delays definitive management. Continuous elevation of the presenting part and rapid delivery is the standard. (Source: RANZCOG: Cord Prolapse)

stem: A 36-year-old G4P2A1 at 32+1 weeks gestation presents to the birth suite with sudden onset of bright red vaginal bleeding approximately 1 hour ago. She reports no pain and no contractions. The bleeding was initially moderate but has now reduced to spotting. Her obstetric history includes two previous lower-segment caesarean sections (the most recent 2 years ago) and a first-trimester miscarriage managed with suction curettage. At her 20-week morphology scan, the placenta was noted to be posterior and covering the internal os completely. A follow-up scan at 28 weeks confirmed a major placenta praevia. Her antenatal course has been otherwise uncomplicated. On examination, her abdomen is soft and non-tender with a singleton foetus in cephalic presentation. The fundal height is consistent with dates. Speculum examination reveals a closed cervix with a small amount of dark blood in the posterior fornix. Foetal cardiotocography shows a baseline of 145 bpm with normal variability, no decelerations, and no uterine activity. Her haemoglobin is 102 g/L (down from 118 g/L at 28 weeks) and haematocrit is 0.31. The estimated blood loss is approximately 400 mL. She is haemodynamically stable with HR 88 bpm and BP 110/70 mmHg. She has received betamethasone 11.4 mg IM 24 hours apart with the second dose given 12 hours ago.
lead_in: Which risk factor in this presentation most strongly raises concern for placenta accreta spectrum disorder over an uncomplicated major placenta praevia?

options.text: Maternal age over 35 years at the time of delivery
options.rationale: Advanced maternal age modestly increases the risk of placenta praevia and accreta, but the magnitude of risk from age alone is far less than that conferred by the combination of praevia with prior uterine surgery. (Source: RANZCOG: Placenta Accreta Spectrum)

options.text: The confirmed persistence of a major placenta praevia at 28 weeks gestation
options.rationale: Persistent major placenta praevia at 28 weeks increases the risk of PAS compared to a resolved praevia, but it is the combination with prior caesarean sections that most strongly predicts accreta. Isolated praevia without prior uterine surgery carries a PAS risk of approximately 3%. (Source: RANZCOG: Placenta Accreta Spectrum)

options.text: First-trimester miscarriage managed with suction curettage in addition to the two prior caesarean sections
options.rationale: While suction curettage does cause endometrial disruption and is an additional risk factor, the dominant driver of PAS risk is the combination of placenta praevia with prior caesarean sections. The curettage adds a minor additional risk but is not the strongest predictor. (Source: RANZCOG: Placenta Accreta Spectrum)

options.text: The combination of major placenta praevia with two previous lower-segment caesarean sections
options.rationale: The risk of placenta accreta spectrum rises dramatically with the number of prior caesarean sections in the setting of placenta praevia. With no prior CS, the risk is approximately 3%; with one prior CS, 11%; with two prior CS, 40%; with three prior CS, 67%. This exponential rise makes the combination the strongest risk factor. (Source: RANZCOG: Placenta Accreta Spectrum; FIGO Consensus Guidelines on PAS)

options.text: The drop in haemoglobin from 118 g/L at 28 weeks to 102 g/L at presentation with bright red bleeding
options.rationale: A drop in haemoglobin reflects blood loss from the current episode of bleeding and is a marker of haemorrhage severity, not a risk factor for PAS. PAS risk is determined by pre-existing uterine factors rather than the clinical presentation of bleeding. (Source: RANZCOG: Placenta Accreta Spectrum)

stem: A 29-year-old primigravida at 40+0 weeks gestation is in the second stage of labour. She has been pushing for 90 minutes with an epidural in situ. Her pregnancy was complicated by diet-controlled gestational diabetes diagnosed at 28 weeks on the 75 g OGTT (fasting 5.3 mmol/L, 1-hour 10.2 mmol/L, 2-hour 8.6 mmol/L). Foetal surveillance has been normal. The head has delivered spontaneously over an intact perineum. Following delivery of the head, the chin retracts tightly against the perineum (turtle-neck sign) and restitution does not occur. Gentle downward traction fails to deliver the anterior shoulder. The accoucheur calls for help. The woman has been moved to the end of the bed and her legs are placed in hyperflexion (McRoberts' position). After one further attempt at downward traction, the anterior shoulder remains undelivered. Suprapubic pressure has been applied by an assistant using a closed fist in a downward and lateral direction with the next contraction. The shoulder has still not delivered. The paediatric team is present and the time since delivery of the head is now 2 minutes and 30 seconds.
lead_in: What is the SINGLE BEST next manoeuvre to attempt?

options.text: Apply firmer downward traction on the foetal head combined with continuous suprapubic pressure for a full 60 seconds
options.rationale: Increasing traction force risks brachial plexus injury and does not address the underlying impaction of the anterior shoulder behind the symphysis pubis. After McRoberts' position and suprapubic pressure have failed, internal manoeuvres are required. (Source: RANZCOG: Shoulder Dystocia)

options.text: Deliver the posterior arm by entering the sacral hollow, locating the humerus, sweeping it across the chest, and delivering the arm
options.rationale: When McRoberts' position and suprapubic pressure fail to resolve shoulder dystocia, delivery of the posterior arm is the next recommended step in the HELPERR mnemonic. This reduces the bisacromial diameter by up to 2 cm and has a high success rate. The procedure involves entering posteriorly along the sacral curve, splinting the humerus, and sweeping the arm across the foetal chest. (Source: RANZCOG: Shoulder Dystocia; KEMH: Shoulder Dystocia Guideline)

options.text: Rotate the foetus by applying pressure to the anterior shoulder through the abdominal wall (Rubin I manoeuvre)
options.rationale: Rubin I (external rotation of the anterior shoulder via abdominal pressure) is a variant of suprapubic pressure and has already been functionally attempted. When external manoeuvres have failed, internal rotational manoeuvres (Rubin II or Woods' screw) or posterior arm delivery are indicated. (Source: RANZCOG: Shoulder Dystocia)

options.text: Perform an episiotomy to increase the available space for delivery before attempting further traction
options.rationale: Shoulder dystocia is a bony impaction problem, not a soft-tissue obstruction. Episiotomy alone will not relieve the impaction of the anterior shoulder behind the symphysis pubis. It may be performed to facilitate access for internal manoeuvres, but it is not a manoeuvre in itself and is not the next priority. (Source: RANZCOG: Shoulder Dystocia)

options.text: Perform the Gaskin all-fours manoeuvre by rolling the woman onto her hands and knees
options.rationale: The Gaskin manoeuvre (all-fours position) can be effective and is sometimes used earlier in the sequence. However, with an epidural in situ, moving the woman to all-fours is difficult and potentially unsafe. In an Australian tertiary setting with epidural, internal manoeuvres (posterior arm delivery or internal rotation) are the next step after failed McRoberts and suprapubic pressure. (Source: RANZCOG: Shoulder Dystocia)

stem: A 24-year-old primigravida at 36+4 weeks gestation is brought to the birth suite by ambulance following a tonic-clonic seizure at home that lasted approximately 2 minutes. She has a known diagnosis of pre-eclampsia with severe features diagnosed 3 days ago, for which she has been receiving oral labetalol 200 mg BD. She had been complaining of a severe frontal headache and epigastric pain since this morning. On arrival, she is postictal and drowsy with a GCS of 13 (E3 V4 M6). Her blood pressure is 175/115 mmHg on the left arm in the semi-recumbent position, confirmed with a repeat reading 15 minutes later. She has 3+ protein on urine dipstick and hyperreflexia with 4 beats of clonus at the ankle. IV access has been established. The obstetric and anaesthetic teams are present, and plans are being made for urgent delivery once she is stabilised. She weighs 72 kg. Her most recent bloods from 2 hours ago show: Hb 108 g/L, platelets 95 x 10^9/L, creatinine 88 micromol/L, ALT 125 U/L, urate 0.48 mmol/L.
lead_in: What is the most appropriate magnesium sulphate regimen for this woman?

options.text: Magnesium sulphate 4 g IV loading dose over 20 minutes, then 1 g/hour IV infusion for 24 hours after delivery or 24 hours after the last seizure, whichever is later
options.rationale: The standard Australian eclampsia regimen is a 4 g IV loading dose over 20 minutes followed by a 1 g/hour maintenance infusion. Therapy is continued for 24 hours after delivery OR 24 hours after the last seizure, whichever occurs later. This is distinct from the pre-eclampsia prophylaxis regimen (which omits the loading dose). (Source: SOMANZ Guideline: Management of Hypertensive Disorders of Pregnancy; KEMH: Eclampsia Guideline)

options.text: Magnesium sulphate 4 g IV loading dose over 5 minutes, then 2 g/hour IV infusion for 24 hours postpartum
options.rationale: A 5-minute loading infusion carries a risk of hypotension and maternal collapse. The standard loading dose is infused over 20 minutes. A maintenance rate of 2 g/hour exceeds the standard Australian regimen of 1 g/hour and increases the risk of magnesium toxicity without demonstrated additional benefit. (Source: SOMANZ: Hypertensive Disorders of Pregnancy)

options.text: Magnesium sulphate 4 g IM loading dose into each buttock (8 g total), then 5 g IM 4-hourly for 24 hours
options.rationale: The IM-only Pritchard regimen is rarely used in contemporary Australian practice. It is painful and absorption is unreliable. The IV regimen is standard of care in Australian tertiary settings with IV access available. (Source: SOMANZ: Hypertensive Disorders of Pregnancy)

options.text: Magnesium sulphate 2 g IV loading dose over 20 minutes, then 1 g/hour IV infusion for 24 hours after the last seizure
options.rationale: A 2 g loading dose is insufficient for eclampsia. The standard eclampsia treatment regimen requires a 4 g loading dose. A 2 g loading dose would be appropriate for seizure prophylaxis in severe pre-eclampsia but is not adequate for treating established eclampsia. (Source: SOMANZ: Hypertensive Disorders of Pregnancy)

options.text: Magnesium sulphate 6 g IV loading dose over 30 minutes, then 1.5 g/hour IV infusion for 48 hours postpartum
options.rationale: A 6 g loading dose exceeds the standard 4 g recommendation and increases toxicity risk without evidence of benefit. The 48-hour duration also exceeds standard recommendations - therapy is typically continued for 24 hours after the last seizure or 24 hours after delivery, whichever is later. (Source: SOMANZ: Hypertensive Disorders of Pregnancy)

stem: A 31-year-old G3P2 at 39+1 weeks gestation has just delivered a 4.2 kg infant vaginally following a labour augmented with oxytocin for 14 hours. The placenta delivered spontaneously 10 minutes later. Her estimated blood loss is now 800 mL and ongoing. The uterus is boggy on palpation and the fundus is above the umbilicus. The accoucheur has commenced uterine fundal massage and the midwife is running an IV infusion of oxytocin 40 units in 1 L of normal saline at 250 mL/hour. The bladder has been emptied with a straight catheter. Despite these measures for 5 minutes, the bleeding continues and the uterus remains atonic. Her current vital signs are HR 112 bpm, BP 100/65 mmHg, RR 20. She has no known drug allergies. Her antenatal haemoglobin 2 weeks ago was 115 g/L. She has a history of asthma well-controlled with salbutamol as needed, with no exacerbations in the past year.
lead_in: Which SINGLE uterotonic agent should be administered next?

options.text: IM syntometrine 1 mL (oxytocin 5 IU + ergometrine 500 mcg) as a single dose
options.rationale: Syntometrine contains ergometrine, which is relatively contraindicated in patients with hypertension (though this woman's current BP is not hypertensive, she is at risk of hypertension from oxytocin and ergometrine). Furthermore, syntometrine is typically used for prophylaxis (third stage) rather than treatment of established PPH. Ergometrine is a second-line agent after oxytocin for PPH treatment, but the woman's asthma is an additional relative contraindication. (Source: KEMH: Postpartum Haemorrhage Guideline; Therapeutic Guidelines: Obstetric Emergencies)

options.text: IM carboprost 250 mcg (Haemabate) every 15 minutes up to a maximum of 8 doses
options.rationale: Carboprost (prostaglandin F2-alpha) is a second-line uterotonic for PPH but is relatively contraindicated in asthma because it can cause significant bronchospasm. In a woman with known asthma, even if well-controlled, it should be used with extreme caution and is generally avoided when alternatives exist. Ergometrine would typically be tried first. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Obstetric Emergencies; KEMH: Postpartum Haemorrhage Guideline)

options.text: IV tranexamic acid 1 g slowly over 10 minutes as a single dose and IV ergometrine 250 mcg slowly
options.rationale: Tranexamic acid (TXA) is indicated in PPH and should be given within 3 hours of bleeding onset. However, TXA is an antifibrinolytic, not a uterotonic. It should be given AS WELL AS a uterotonic, not as the sole next agent. The combination here includes ergometrine which is appropriate, but the question asks for the SINGLE next agent. The priority is to give a second-line uterotonic; TXA is an adjunct. (Source: WOMAN Trial; KEMH: Postpartum Haemorrhage Guideline)

options.text: IV ergometrine 250 mcg slowly over 1-2 minutes, repeated once after 5 minutes if needed
options.rationale: Ergometrine is the standard second-line uterotonic in PPH after oxytocin, provided there are no contraindications. The main contraindications are hypertension (current BP normal at 100/65) and cardiac disease. Asthma is a relative contraindication but is not absolute - ergometrine can cause bronchospasm, but in a well-controlled asthmatic with no recent exacerbations, the benefit of controlling life-threatening haemorrhage outweighs this risk. Carboprost would be more problematic in asthma. (Source: KEMH: Postpartum Haemorrhage Guideline; Therapeutic Guidelines: Obstetric Emergencies)

options.text: Misoprostol 800 mcg per rectum as a single dose
options.rationale: Rectal misoprostol has a slower onset of action compared to parenteral uterotonics and is not the recommended second-line agent in the acute management of PPH when IV access is available and the haemorrhage is ongoing. It may be used when other uterotonics are contraindicated or unavailable, but ergometrine is the appropriate next step here. (Source: KEMH: Postpartum Haemorrhage Guideline)

stem: A 27-year-old G2P1 at 28+3 weeks gestation presents to the emergency department reporting a sudden gush of clear fluid from her vagina approximately 2 hours ago while sitting at her desk. She has since noted ongoing trickling of fluid. She reports no pain, no contractions, and normal foetal movements. Her pregnancy has been uncomplicated to date with a normal morphology scan at 20 weeks. On examination, her temperature is 37.9°C, HR 92 bpm, BP 110/68 mmHg. The abdomen is soft and non-tender, fundal height is 28 cm. On speculum examination, a pool of clear fluid is seen in the posterior fornix with a positive nitrazine test. The cervix is closed and long on visual inspection. A foetal fibronectin swab has been taken (result pending) and a high vaginal swab has been sent for microscopy and culture. Cardiotocography shows a baseline of 150 bpm with moderate variability, no decelerations, and irregular tightenings every 10-12 minutes. Her most recent cervical length at 20 weeks was 42 mm. She received a full course of betamethasone for threatened preterm labour at 26 weeks (which resolved spontaneously).
lead_in: What is the most appropriate management plan?

options.text: Admit for inpatient monitoring, administer a rescue course of betamethasone (11.4 mg IM, two doses 24 hours apart), commence oral azithromycin 1 g stat and ampicillin 2 g IV 6-hourly for 48 hours, then amoxicillin 250 mg PO 8-hourly for 5 days
options.rationale: A rescue course of corticosteroids is indicated when the prior course was given more than 7 days ago and delivery is anticipated within the next 7 days. Here the prior course was at 26 weeks, now 28+3 weeks, which is more than 7 days, so a rescue course is indicated. However, the temperature of 37.9°C is a red flag for possible chorioamnionitis; the question becomes whether delivery rather than conservative management is indicated. (Source: RANZCOG: PPROM Guideline; KEMH: PPROM Guideline)

options.text: Induction of labour with intravenous oxytocin, as PPROM at 28+3 weeks with a temperature of 37.9°C mandates delivery within 24 hours
options.rationale: A single temperature of 37.9°C in an otherwise well woman with no other signs of chorioamnionitis (no uterine tenderness, normal foetal heart rate pattern, no purulent discharge) does not mandate immediate delivery. A temperature of 37.9°C is a borderline finding; serial observations and inflammatory markers are required to diagnose chorioamnionitis. This temperature alone is not a delivery trigger. (Source: RANZCOG: PPROM Guideline; KEMH: PPROM Guideline)

options.text: Admit for conservative management with 4-hourly observations (temperature, HR, BP), twice-weekly CTG, weekly bloods (WCC, CRP), administer a rescue course of betamethasone, commence oral erythromycin 250 mg QID for 10 days, and plan delivery at 34+0 weeks if undelivered
options.rationale: This woman is at 28+3 weeks, PPROM has been confirmed, she is clinically stable with no clear signs of chorioamnionitis. The standard approach is conservative management with close surveillance for chorioamnionitis (4-hourly temp, HR, twice-weekly CTG, weekly inflammatory markers). A rescue course of betamethasone is indicated (prior course >7 days ago). Erythromycin 250 mg QID for 10 days is the recommended latency antibiotic in Australian practice. Delivery is planned at 34+0 weeks if undelivered. The borderline temperature of 37.9°C warrants close observation but is not yet an indication for delivery. (Source: RANZCOG: PPROM Guideline; KEMH: PPROM Guideline)

options.text: Administer a single rescue dose of betamethasone 11.4 mg IM, observe for 4 hours, and discharge home with strict return precautions if the temperature normalises and there are no signs of chorioamnionitis
options.rationale: Outpatient management of PPROM is not recommended. The risk of ascending infection, cord prolapse, and placental abruption requires inpatient monitoring with regular observations. Discharge home, even with strict return precautions, is not standard care for PPROM at any gestation. (Source: RANZCOG: PPROM Guideline)

options.text: Admit for inpatient monitoring, administer a rescue course of betamethasone, commence IV ampicillin 2 g 6-hourly and oral clarithromycin 500 mg BD for 10 days, and plan delivery at 37+0 weeks if undelivered
options.rationale: This plan uses ampicillin/clarithromycin instead of the Australian-recommended erythromycin regimen (based on the ORACLE trial, erythromycin alone is recommended; co-amoxiclav is associated with increased NEC risk). Additionally, delivery at 37+0 weeks is too conservative - PPROM is typically delivered by 34+0 weeks in Australian practice (the risk of chorioamnionitis after 34 weeks begins to outweigh the prematurity risk). (Source: KEMH: PPROM Guideline; RANZCOG: PPROM Guideline)

stem: A 29-year-old primigravida at 29+5 weeks gestation is being managed as an inpatient for early-onset foetal growth restriction. Her pregnancy is otherwise uncomplicated with a normal morphology scan at 20 weeks. She has no significant past medical history and is a non-smoker. Serial growth ultrasounds have shown an estimated foetal weight consistently below the 3rd centile since 24 weeks with a falling growth velocity. Umbilical artery Doppler studies today show absent end-diastolic flow. Previous Dopplers 2 weeks ago showed a raised umbilical artery pulsatility index (>95th centile) but positive end-diastolic flow. Middle cerebral artery Doppler today shows a low pulsatility index consistent with brain-sparing. The ductus venosus Doppler shows a positive a-wave. The amniotic fluid index is 7.5 cm (borderline oligohydramnios). CTG today shows a baseline of 145 bpm with reduced variability (3-5 bpm) and occasional spontaneous accelerations, but no decelerations. The woman has received a full course of betamethasone (two doses 24 hours apart) completed yesterday. Her blood pressure is 120/78 mmHg, and urinalysis shows no protein. She is on twice-daily CTG monitoring and has been counselled that delivery is likely within the next 48 hours. The neonatal team has reviewed her. Magnesium sulphate for foetal neuroprotection has not yet been commenced.
lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.text: Commence IV magnesium sulphate 4 g loading dose for foetal neuroprotection and plan for delivery within 24 hours given the AEDF and reduced CTG variability
options.rationale: AEDF with reduced CTG variability indicates foetal compromise that warrants expedited delivery. However, delivery is typically planned within 24-48 hours rather than urgently (hours), provided the ductus venosus a-wave remains positive and CTG shows no decelerations. Immediate delivery (within hours) is indicated when AEDF is accompanied by abnormal DV Doppler (absent/reversed a-wave) or recurrent decelerations. Magnesium sulphate for neuroprotection should be commenced, but the timing of delivery for this specific combination is 24-48 hours. (Source: RANZCOG: Foetal Growth Restriction Guideline; KEMH: FGR Guideline)

options.text: Commence IV magnesium sulphate 4 g loading dose over 20 minutes followed by 1 g/hour maintenance, and plan delivery within 24-48 hours with continuous intrapartum CTG monitoring
options.rationale: In FGR at 29+5 weeks with AEDF, brain-sparing, borderline AFI, and reduced CTG variability (but no decelerations and DV a-wave positive), delivery is indicated within 24-48 hours. Magnesium sulphate for foetal neuroprotection (4 g IV loading dose over 20 minutes, then 1 g/hour maintenance) should be commenced, given the gestation is <30 weeks and delivery is imminent. The positive DV a-wave and absence of CTG decelerations allow for this timeframe without requiring immediate emergency delivery. (Source: RANZCOG: Foetal Growth Restriction Guideline; KEMH: FGR Guideline)

options.text: Perform immediate caesarean section as the AEDF and reduced CTG variability indicate acute-on-chronic foetal compromise requiring delivery within 2-4 hours
options.rationale: AEDF with reduced variability does NOT meet criteria for immediate delivery (Category 1 caesarean). Immediate delivery is indicated for recurrent decelerations, reversed end-diastolic flow, or abnormal ductus venosus Doppler (absent/reversed a-wave). In the presence of a positive DV a-wave and no decelerations, delivery can be planned within 24-48 hours with close monitoring. (Source: RANZCOG: Foetal Growth Restriction Guideline)

options.text: Perform a repeat CTG in 12 hours and only plan delivery if the CTG deteriorates to show recurrent decelerations or the ductus venosus a-wave becomes absent or reversed
options.rationale: AEDF with reduced CTG variability is an indication for delivery, not for continued observation. Expectant management beyond 48 hours is not appropriate once these features are present. Delivery should be planned within 24-48 hours, not deferred until further deterioration. (Source: RANZCOG: Foetal Growth Restriction Guideline)

options.text: Administer a rescue course of betamethasone (two further doses 24 hours apart) and plan for delivery at 32+0 weeks, repeating umbilical artery Dopplers twice weekly
options.rationale: A rescue course of corticosteroids is indicated if >7 days have elapsed since the previous course. This course was completed YESTERDAY, so a rescue course is not appropriate at this point. Furthermore, AEDF with reduced CTG variability mandates delivery within 24-48 hours, not at 32+0 weeks. Continued expectant management with twice-weekly Dopplers is not safe. (Source: RANZCOG: Foetal Growth Restriction Guideline)
